1:
"This second epistle, beloved, I now write unto you; in both which I stir up your pure minds by way of remembrance:" The witnesses note the purpose of the epistle — to stir up the saints by remembrance. Gill notes this is a transition to another part of the epistle; having largely described the false teachers, the secret enemies of the faith, the apostle passes on to the more open adversaries and profane scoffers. The witnesses note Peter declaring that this is the second epistle he writes, in both stirring up their pure minds by way of remembrance; the apostle's aim to awaken the saints' memory of revealed truth — that Peter, opening the last part of his letter, tells his loving purpose: "this second epistle, beloved, I now write unto you" (a second letter to the same beloved saints) "in both which I stir up your pure minds by way of remembrance"; the witnesses note the gracious method — Peter does not assume the saints are ignorant, but "stir[s] up" their "pure minds" (sincere, uncorrupted understandings) "by way of remembrance," recalling to their memory the truths they already knew; so that even established believers need their minds awakened and stirred to keep firm hold of the doctrines they have received; in both which I stir up your pure minds by way of remembrance. The witnesses note this second epistle, beloved, I now write unto you. The witnesses note in both which I stir up your pure minds by way of remembrance.

The witnesses note Peter's aim — to stir up the saints' pure minds by way of remembrance; even established believers needing their memory awakened to keep firm hold of received truth.

So the witnesses make the verse the stirring up of the saints by remembrance. The lesson the expositors draw is that Peter writes to awaken the believers' minds to the truths they already knew. The witnesses note the second epistle: "this second epistle, beloved, I now write unto you" — Gill notes a second letter to the beloved; the second epistle; the the beloved; the the same saints; the writes; the a second. The expositors observe the transition: a transition to the open scoffers; the transition; the the scoffers; the the adversaries; the open; the passes on. The witnesses press the stir up: "I stir up your pure minds" — awaken their understandings; the stir up; the awaken; the their minds; the stir; the rouse. The expositors draw the lesson on the pure minds: "pure minds" — sincere, uncorrupted understandings; the pure minds; the sincere; the uncorrupted; the understandings; the clear. The witnesses observe the by remembrance: "by way of remembrance" — recalling truths they knew; the by remembrance; the recalling; the truths; the they knew; the memory. The expositors note the established need stirring: even established believers need their minds stirred; the established need stirring; the the believers; the firm hold; the the doctrines; the received. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the this second epistle beloved I now write unto you, the in both which I stir up your pure minds by way of remembrance, and the stirring up by remembrance. The verse thus records the stirring up of the saints by remembrance — teaching that Peter writes to awaken the believers' minds to the truths they already knew.
2:
"That ye may be mindful of the words which were spoken before by the holy prophets, and of the commandment of us the apostles of the Lord and Saviour:" The witnesses note the truths the saints are to remember — the prophets' words and the apostles' commandment. Barnes notes "that ye may be mindful of the words" — of the doctrines, the truths, the prophetic statements; Jude says the same was foretold by the apostles. The witnesses note Peter naming the end of his writing — that they be mindful of the words of the holy prophets and the commandment of the apostles; the joining of prophets and apostles as the twofold authority to be remembered — that Peter names the great body of truth he would have them keep in mind: "the words which were spoken before by the holy prophets" (the Old Testament Scriptures and prophecies) "and of the commandment of us the apostles of the Lord and Saviour" (the New Testament teaching of the apostles, Christ's own sent ones); the witnesses note the joining of these two — the prophets and the apostles, the Old and New Testament witness, set side by side as one harmonious authority; so that the believer's stability lies in holding fast both the ancient prophets' words and the apostles' commandment, which together make up the revealed truth of God; the words which were spoken before by the holy prophets, and of the commandment of us the apostles. The witnesses note the words which were spoken before by the holy prophets. The witnesses note and of the commandment of us the apostles of the Lord and Saviour.

The witnesses note the twofold authority to be remembered — the words of the holy prophets and the commandment of the apostles; the Old and New Testament witness set side by side as one.

So the witnesses make the verse the joining of prophets and apostles. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer is to keep in mind both the prophets' words and the apostles' commandment, one harmonious authority. The witnesses note the be mindful: "that ye may be mindful of the words" — Barnes notes the doctrines and truths; the be mindful; the the words; the the doctrines; the the truths; the remember. The expositors observe the holy prophets: "spoken before by the holy prophets" — the Old Testament prophecies; the holy prophets; the the Old Testament; the the prophecies; the the Scriptures; the foretold. The witnesses press the the commandment: "the commandment of us the apostles" — the apostles' New Testament teaching; the the commandment; the the apostles; the the New Testament; the teaching; the Christ's sent ones. The expositors draw the lesson on the of the Lord and Saviour: "of the Lord and Saviour" — the apostles of Christ; the of the Lord and Saviour; the the apostles; the of Christ; the sent; the authority. The witnesses observe the joining the two: prophets and apostles set side by side; the joining the two; the prophets; the apostles; the side by side; the one authority. The expositors note the one authority: together they make one harmonious authority; the one authority; the harmonious; the the revealed truth; the of God; the together. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the words which were spoken before by the holy prophets, the and of the commandment of us the apostles of the Lord and Saviour, and the twofold authority. The verse thus records the joining of prophets and apostles — teaching that the believer is to keep in mind both the prophets' words and the apostles' commandment.
3:
"Knowing this first, that there shall come in the last days scoffers, walking after their own lusts," The witnesses note the foretold coming of scoffers in the last days. Matthew Henry notes that, to quicken and excite us to a serious minding of what God has revealed by the prophets and apostles, we are told there will be scoffers; Gill notes this might easily be known from the writings of the apostles and prophets. The witnesses note Peter declaring that, as a first thing to be known, there shall come in the last days scoffers walking after their own lusts; the foretelling of last-days scoffers driven by their own lusts — that Peter, that the saints be forearmed, foretells what they must expect: "knowing this first" (as a chief and certain thing) "that there shall come in the last days scoffers" (mockers who deride the faith, and especially the hope of Christ's coming) "walking after their own lusts"; the witnesses note the root of their scoffing — these mockers are driven not by honest reason but by "their own lusts," the sinful desires they will not give up; for it is the love of sin that makes men scoff at the truth and the judgment to come, that they may sin without fear; so that the rise of such scoffers is no surprise but a thing foretold, and their mockery springs from their own corruption; there shall come in the last days scoffers, walking after their own lusts. The witnesses note knowing this first, that there shall come in the last days scoffers. The witnesses note walking after their own lusts.

The witnesses note the foretold last-days scoffers — mockers driven by their own lusts; the love of sin making men scoff at the truth, that they may sin without fear; their rise no surprise but foretold.

So the witnesses make the verse the foretelling of last-days scoffers. The lesson the expositors draw is that scoffers driven by their own lusts will arise in the last days, as foretold, their mockery springing from corruption. The witnesses note the knowing this first: "knowing this first" — Gill notes a chief and certain thing; the knowing this first; the chief; the certain; the foretold; the known. The expositors observe Matthew Henry's note: Henry notes we are told there will be scoffers, to quicken us; the the scoffers; the foretold; the quicken; the excite; the warned. The witnesses press the last days: "there shall come in the last days scoffers" — mockers in the last days; the last days; the scoffers; the mockers; the deride; the the faith. The expositors draw the lesson on the deride the coming: especially deriding the hope of Christ's coming; the deride the coming; the the hope; the Christ's coming; the mock; the the judgment. The witnesses observe the their own lusts: "walking after their own lusts" — driven by their sinful desires; the their own lusts; the sinful desires; the driven; the the root; the corruption. The expositors note the sin without fear: the love of sin makes them scoff, that they may sin freely; the sin without fear; the the love of sin; the scoff; the freely; the corruption. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the knowing this first that there shall come in the last days scoffers, the walking after their own lusts, and the foretold scoffers. The verse thus records the foretelling of last-days scoffers — teaching that scoffers driven by their own lusts will arise, as foretold, their mockery springing from corruption.
4:
"And saying, Where is the promise of his coming? for since the fathers fell asleep, all things continue as they were from the beginning of the creation." The witnesses note the scoffers' argument against Christ's coming. Gill notes the object of their scorn is the coming of the Lord and Saviour, whom through contempt they do not name; the meaning is, what is become of the promise of his coming, where is its accomplishment? The witnesses note Peter reporting the scoffers' taunt — "Where is the promise of his coming? since the fathers fell asleep, all things continue as they were"; the scoffers' argument from the seeming delay and constancy of nature — that Peter sets out the very words of the mockers' scoff: "Where is the promise of his coming?" (a sneer at the long delay — where is this coming so often promised yet not come?) "for since the fathers fell asleep" (since the first generations died) "all things continue as they were from the beginning of the creation"; the witnesses note the substance of their reasoning — they argue from the apparent unbroken constancy of the natural order (the world goes on as ever, nothing changes) that no great divine intervention or coming in judgment is to be expected; so that the scoffers deny Christ's coming on the ground that the course of nature has never been interrupted and never will be; Where is the promise of his coming? The witnesses note Where is the promise of his coming? The witnesses note since the fathers fell asleep, all things continue as they were from the beginning of the creation.

The witnesses note the scoffers' taunt — "Where is the promise of his coming?" — arguing from the seeming delay and the unbroken constancy of nature that no coming in judgment is to be expected.

So the witnesses make the verse the scoffers' argument from nature's constancy. The lesson the expositors draw is that the mockers deny Christ's coming on the ground that nature has never been interrupted. The witnesses note the where is the promise: "Where is the promise of his coming?" — Gill notes a sneer at the delay; the where is the promise; the the sneer; the the delay; the his coming; the mock. The expositors observe the not name him: through contempt they do not name him; the not name him; the contempt; the the Saviour; the scorn; the mock. The witnesses press the fathers fell asleep: "since the fathers fell asleep" — since the first generations died; the fathers fell asleep; the the generations; the died; the the first; the long ago. The expositors draw the lesson on the all things continue: "all things continue as they were" — the world goes on unchanged; the all things continue; the unchanged; the the world; the goes on; the constant. The witnesses observe the nature's constancy: they argue from nature's unbroken constancy; the nature's constancy; the unbroken; the the natural order; the never changes; the constant. The expositors note the no intervention: that no divine intervention is to be expected; the no intervention; the no coming; the in judgment; the not expected; the deny. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Where is the promise of his coming, the since the fathers fell asleep all things continue as they were from the beginning of the creation, and the scoffers' argument. The verse thus records the scoffers' argument from nature's constancy — teaching that the mockers deny Christ's coming on the ground that nature has never been interrupted.
5:
"For this they willingly are ignorant of, that by the word of God the heavens were of old, and the earth standing out of the water and in the water:" The witnesses note the scoffers' wilful ignorance of the creation by God's word. Gill notes these men, having professed Christianity and had the Scriptures, might have known that the heavens and earth were from the beginning, made by the word of God; their ignorance was wilful. The witnesses note Peter declaring that the scoffers willingly are ignorant that by the word of God the heavens were of old, and the earth standing out of and in the water; the wilful ignorance that forgets creation by God's word — that Peter answers the scoffers by exposing their wilful blindness to a great fact: "for this they willingly are ignorant of" (it is no honest mistake; they deliberately shut their eyes to it) — namely, "that by the word of God the heavens were of old, and the earth standing out of the water and in the water"; the witnesses note the point of the argument — the same God who, by his bare word, made the heavens and formed the earth out of and amid the waters, is no idle spectator bound by nature's fixed course; the scoffers conveniently forget that the world they call unchangeable was itself called into being by God's word, and so is wholly at his command; for this they willingly are ignorant of. The witnesses note for this they willingly are ignorant of. The witnesses note that by the word of God the heavens were of old, and the earth standing out of the water and in the water.

The witnesses note the scoffers' wilful ignorance — deliberately forgetting that the heavens and earth were made by God's word; the world they call unchangeable itself called into being by God.

So the witnesses make the verse the wilful ignorance that forgets creation. The lesson the expositors draw is that the scoffers deliberately ignore that God made the heavens and earth by his word, the world wholly at his command. The witnesses note the willingly ignorant: "for this they willingly are ignorant of" — Gill notes a deliberate, wilful ignorance; the willingly ignorant; the deliberate; the wilful; the shut their eyes; the no honest mistake. The expositors observe the they might have known: having the Scriptures, they might have known; the they might have known; the the Scriptures; the professed; the the creation; the known. The witnesses press the by the word of God: "by the word of God the heavens were of old" — made by God's bare word; the by the word of God; the bare word; the the heavens; the made; the of old. The expositors draw the lesson on the the earth: "the earth standing out of the water and in the water" — formed out of and amid the waters; the the earth; the out of the water; the in the water; the formed; the the creation. The witnesses observe the no idle spectator: the Creator is no idle spectator bound by nature; the no idle spectator; the the Creator; the bound by nature; the at his command; the sovereign. The expositors note the called into being: the world they call unchangeable was called into being by God; the called into being; the unchangeable; the by God; the his word; the command. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for this they willingly are ignorant of, the that by the word of God the heavens were of old and the earth standing out of the water and in the water, and the wilful ignorance. The verse thus records the wilful ignorance that forgets creation — teaching that the scoffers deliberately ignore that God made the heavens and earth by his word.
6:
"Whereby the world that then was, being overflowed with water, perished:" The witnesses note that the old world perished by the flood. Gill notes "the world that then was" is the old world, the world before the flood, which had stood from the creation 1656 years; this world, being overflowed with water, perished. The witnesses note Peter declaring that the world that then was, being overflowed with water, perished; the flood as proof that the world's order has indeed been broken by God — that Peter drives home the answer to the scoffers' claim that "all things continue as they were": they are wrong, for once already God broke the course of nature in judgment — "whereby the world that then was, being overflowed with water, perished"; the witnesses note the force — by the same "word of God" and the same waters out of which the earth was formed, the old world (the world of Noah's day) was destroyed in the great flood; so the scoffers' premise that nature's course has never been interrupted is false: God did once judge the world by water, and the very Scriptures the scoffers profess record it; so that the flood stands as the standing witness that God can and does break in upon the world in judgment; whereby the world that then was, being overflowed with water, perished. The witnesses note whereby the world that then was, being overflowed with water. The witnesses note perished.

The witnesses note the old world perishing by the flood — God once breaking the course of nature in judgment; the scoffers' premise false, for the very Scriptures they profess record the flood.

So the witnesses make the verse the flood as proof of God's judgment. The lesson the expositors draw is that the old world perished by the flood, proving God has indeed broken nature's course in judgment. The witnesses note the world that then was: "the world that then was" — Gill notes the old world before the flood; the world that then was; the the old world; the before the flood; the Noah's day; the the creation. The expositors observe the overflowed: "being overflowed with water" — destroyed by the great flood; the overflowed; the the flood; the the waters; the destroyed; the the deluge. The witnesses press the perished: "perished" — the old world perished; the perished; the destroyed; the the old world; the judged; the gone. The expositors draw the lesson on the by the same word: by the same word of God that made it; the by the same word; the of God; the made it; the destroyed; the the waters. The witnesses observe the scoffers' premise false: the scoffers' premise that nature is unbroken is false; the premise false; the nature unbroken; the false; the once judged; the broken. The expositors note the the Scriptures record it: the very Scriptures the scoffers profess record the flood; the the Scriptures; the record it; the the flood; the profess; the witness. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the whereby the world that then was being overflowed with water, the perished, and the flood as proof. The verse thus records the flood as proof of God's judgment — teaching that the old world perished by the flood, proving God has broken nature's course in judgment.
7:
"But the heavens and the earth, which are now, by the same word are kept in store, reserved unto fire against the day of judgment and perdition of ungodly men." The witnesses note that the present world is reserved unto fire for the day of judgment. Gill notes "the heavens and the earth which are now" are in being, in distinction from the heavens that were of old, and the world that then was when the flood overflowed it. The witnesses note Peter declaring that the present heavens and earth, by the same word, are kept in store, reserved unto fire against the day of judgment and perdition of the ungodly; the present world reserved by God's word for a coming judgment by fire — that Peter completes the parallel with the flood: as the old world perished by water, so the present world is appointed to perish by fire — "the heavens and the earth, which are now, by the same word" (the same word of God that made and once flooded the world) "are kept in store, reserved unto fire against the day of judgment and perdition of ungodly men"; the witnesses note the certainty and the design — the world is not left to drift on for ever, but is being "kept in store," reserved by God's word, like fuel laid up, for a fixed "day of judgment" when it shall be destroyed by fire and the ungodly perish; so that the very constancy the scoffers trust in is but God's patient preserving of the world for the appointed day of fiery judgment; reserved unto fire against the day of judgment and perdition of ungodly men. The witnesses note the heavens and the earth, which are now, by the same word are kept in store. The witnesses note reserved unto fire against the day of judgment and perdition of ungodly men.

The witnesses note the present world reserved by God's word unto fire — as the old world perished by water, so this is kept in store for a fixed day of judgment when the ungodly shall perish.

So the witnesses make the verse the world reserved for fiery judgment. The lesson the expositors draw is that the present world is kept by God's word, reserved unto fire for the day of judgment. The witnesses note the which are now: "the heavens and the earth, which are now" — Gill notes the present world, distinct from the old; the which are now; the the present world; the distinct; the the old; the in being. The expositors observe the by the same word: "by the same word are kept in store" — kept by the same word that made it; the by the same word; the kept; the in store; the made it; the preserved. The witnesses press the reserved unto fire: "reserved unto fire" — appointed to perish by fire; the reserved unto fire; the appointed; the by fire; the perish; the kept. The expositors draw the lesson on the day of judgment: "against the day of judgment" — for a fixed day of judgment; the day of judgment; the fixed; the appointed; the the day; the coming. The witnesses observe the perdition of the ungodly: "and perdition of ungodly men" — when the ungodly shall perish; the perdition of the ungodly; the perish; the the ungodly; the destroyed; the judged. The expositors note the patient preserving: the constancy is God's patient preserving for the day; the patient preserving; the for the day; the kept. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the reserved unto fire against the day of judgment and perdition of ungodly men, and the world reserved for fire. The verse thus records the world reserved for fiery judgment — teaching that the present world is kept by God's word, reserved unto fire for the day of judgment.
8:
"But, beloved, be not ignorant of this one thing, that one day is with the Lord as a thousand years, and a thousand years as one day." The witnesses note the truth of God's relation to time, against the charge of delay. Gill notes the apostle addresses the saints he writes to, for whom he had a tender affection, lest they be stumbled at the length of time since the promise of Christ's coming, which the scoffers object. The witnesses note Peter charging the beloved not to be ignorant that one day is with the Lord as a thousand years, and a thousand years as one day; the eternal God to whom no length of time is long or short — that Peter, against the scoffers' taunt of delay (v.4), reminds the saints of God's relation to time: "be not ignorant of this one thing, that one day is with the Lord as a thousand years, and a thousand years as one day"; the witnesses note the great truth — God is eternal, dwelling above time, so that to him the longest span and the shortest are alike; what seems a long delay to short-lived men is nothing to the everlasting God, for whom a thousand years pass as a day and a single day holds the fulness of a thousand years; so that the seeming slowness of Christ's coming is no real delay with God, who measures not by man's impatient clock but by his own eternal counsel; one day is with the Lord as a thousand years, and a thousand years as one day. The witnesses note one day is with the Lord as a thousand years. The witnesses note and a thousand years as one day.

The witnesses note the eternal God to whom no time is long or short — a thousand years as a day; what seems long delay to men is nothing to the everlasting God, who measures by his own counsel.

So the witnesses make the verse God's eternal relation to time. The lesson the expositors draw is that to the eternal God the longest and shortest spans are alike, so the seeming delay is no real delay. The witnesses note the be not ignorant: "be not ignorant of this one thing" — Gill notes lest they stumble at the length of time; the be not ignorant; the lest they stumble; the the length; the the delay; the comforted. The expositors observe the one day as a thousand years: "one day is with the Lord as a thousand years" — to God a day is as long ages; the one day as a thousand years; the to God; the a day; the long ages; the eternal. The witnesses press the thousand years as one day: "and a thousand years as one day" — and long ages as a single day; the thousand years as one day; the long ages; the a single day; the eternal; the alike. The expositors draw the lesson on the eternal God: God is eternal, above time; the eternal God; the above time; the everlasting; the dwelling; the alike. The witnesses observe the seems long to men: what seems long delay to short-lived men is nothing to God; the seems long to men; the short-lived; the nothing to God; the the delay; the impatient. The expositors note the no real delay: the seeming slowness is no real delay with God; the no real delay; the with God; the eternal. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and a thousand years as one day, and God's relation to time. The verse thus records God's eternal relation to time — teaching that to the eternal God the longest and shortest spans are alike, so the seeming delay is no real delay.
9:
"The Lord is not slack concerning his promise, as some men count slackness; but is longsuffering to us-ward, not willing that any should perish, but that all should come to repentance." The witnesses note that God's seeming delay is his longsuffering toward his own. Gill notes the words regard the particular promise of Christ's coming; the Lord is not slack, but longsuffering to his elect, whom he bears with long before conversion, not willing that any of them should perish. The witnesses note Peter declaring that the Lord is not slack concerning his promise, but is longsuffering toward us, not willing that any should perish, but that all should come to repentance; the longsuffering of God that delays judgment for the sake of repentance — that Peter gives the true reason for the delay the scoffers mock: "the Lord is not slack concerning his promise, as some men count slackness" (it is not negligence or failure to keep his word) "but is longsuffering to us-ward, not willing that any should perish, but that all should come to repentance"; the witnesses note the debate on "any" and "all" — the Reformed taking it of God's elect ("to us-ward"), whom God patiently waits to bring to repentance, not willing that any of them perish, so that the delay is the very space of their salvation; while others read it of a more general, sincere will that none perish but all repent; both agree the delay springs from God's saving longsuffering, that men may come to repentance; not willing that any should perish, but that all should come to repentance. The witnesses note the Lord is not slack concerning his promise, as some men count slackness. The witnesses note not willing that any should perish, but that all should come to repentance.

The witnesses note God's seeming delay as his longsuffering — not slack but patient, not willing that any perish but that all come to repentance; the delay the very space of salvation.

So the witnesses make the verse God's longsuffering that delays for repentance. The lesson the expositors draw is that God is not slack but longsuffering, delaying judgment that men may come to repentance. The witnesses note the not slack: "the Lord is not slack concerning his promise" — Gill notes not negligence or failure; the not slack; the not negligence; the not failure; the his promise; the keeps it. The expositors observe the as some count slackness: "as some men count slackness" — not as the scoffers count it; the as some count slackness; the the scoffers; the count it; the not slack; the mock. The witnesses press the longsuffering: "but is longsuffering to us-ward" — patient toward us; the longsuffering; the patient; the toward us; the his own; the waits. The witnesses present the debate: the witnesses note the debate on "any" and "all" — the Reformed take it of God's elect ("to us-ward"), patiently waited for unto repentance; others read a more general sincere will that none perish; both agree the delay springs from God's saving longsuffering. The expositors draw the lesson on the not perish: "not willing that any should perish" — not willing any be lost; the not perish; the not lost; the any; the saved; the willed. The witnesses observe the come to repentance: "but that all should come to repentance" — that all come to repentance; the come to repentance; the all; the the space; the salvation. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the not willing that any should perish but that all should come to repentance, and God's longsuffering. The verse thus records God's longsuffering that delays for repentance — teaching that God is not slack but patient, delaying judgment that men may come to repentance.
10:
"But the day of the Lord will come as a thief in the night; in the which the heavens shall pass away with a great noise, and the elements shall melt with fervent heat, the earth also and the works that are therein shall be burned up." The witnesses note the sudden coming of the day of the Lord and the dissolution of all things. Gill notes the Lord will come in that day which he has fixed, according to his promise, than which nothing is more certain; and he will come as a thief in the night, suddenly and unexpectedly. The witnesses note Peter declaring that the day of the Lord will come as a thief in the night, in which the heavens shall pass away with a great noise, the elements melt with fervent heat, and the earth be burned up; the certain, sudden day of the Lord and the fiery dissolution of the world — that Peter, having shown the delay to be longsuffering, affirms the certainty and manner of the end: "but the day of the Lord will come" (it surely will come, however delayed) "as a thief in the night" (suddenly, unexpectedly, when men look not for it) — and he paints the awful dissolution: "the heavens shall pass away with a great noise, and the elements shall melt with fervent heat, the earth also and the works that are therein shall be burned up"; the witnesses note the scene of the great day — the whole frame of the visible universe consumed by fire, the heavens vanishing with a roar, the very elements melting, and the earth with all man's works burned up; so that the world the scoffers thought unchangeable shall be utterly dissolved in fire at the sudden coming of the Lord; the day of the Lord will come as a thief in the night. The witnesses note the day of the Lord will come as a thief in the night. The witnesses note the heavens shall pass away with a great noise, and the elements shall melt with fervent heat.

The witnesses note the sudden day of the Lord — coming as a thief, in which the heavens pass away with a great noise, the elements melt, and the earth is burned up; the world utterly dissolved in fire.

So the witnesses make the verse the sudden day and the fiery dissolution. The lesson the expositors draw is that the day of the Lord will come suddenly, when the heavens, elements, and earth shall be dissolved by fire. The witnesses note the will come: "the day of the Lord will come" — Gill notes it surely will come, however delayed; the will come; the certain; the fixed. The expositors observe the as a thief: "as a thief in the night" — suddenly, unexpectedly; the as a thief; the suddenly; the unlooked-for. The witnesses press the heavens pass away: "the heavens shall pass away with a great noise" — vanishing with a roar; the heavens pass away; the a roar; the dissolved. The expositors draw the lesson on the elements melt: "the elements shall melt with fervent heat" — the very elements melt; the elements melt; the fervent heat; the consumed. The witnesses observe the earth burned up: "the earth also and the works that are therein shall be burned up" — the earth and man's works burned; the earth burned up; the burned; the consumed. The expositors note the utterly dissolved: the world the scoffers thought unchangeable utterly dissolved in fire; the utterly dissolved; the in fire; the consumed. The verse thus records the sudden day and the fiery dissolution — teaching that the day of the Lord will come suddenly, when the heavens, elements, and earth shall be dissolved by fire.
11:
"Seeing then that all these things shall be dissolved, what manner of persons ought ye to be in all holy conversation and godliness," The witnesses note the practical use of the world's dissolution — holy living. Matthew Henry notes the apostle, having instructed them in the doctrine of the last things, applies it; and Gill notes that since nothing is more certain than the dissolution of all things by fire, this should engage to holiness. The witnesses note Peter asking, seeing all these things shall be dissolved, what manner of persons they ought to be in all holy conduct and godliness; the holy living that the certain dissolution of the world should produce — that Peter, from the great fact of the coming dissolution, draws the searching practical question: "seeing then that all these things shall be dissolved" (since the whole visible world is destined to be melted and burned up) "what manner of persons ought ye to be in all holy conversation and godliness"; the witnesses note the force of the appeal — if all earthly things are so soon to pass away in fire, how loose should the believer sit to them, and how earnestly should he give himself to "holy conversation and godliness," a life wholly set apart to God; so that the doctrine of the end is no idle speculation but a powerful spur to holiness — the certainty that this world is passing should make the Christian a holy, heavenly-minded man; what manner of persons ought ye to be in all holy conversation and godliness. The witnesses note seeing then that all these things shall be dissolved. The witnesses note what manner of persons ought ye to be in all holy conversation and godliness.

The witnesses note the holy living the world's dissolution should produce — if all earthly things pass in fire, how loose the believer should sit to them, giving himself to holy conduct and godliness.

So the witnesses make the verse the holy living that the dissolution should produce. The lesson the expositors draw is that, since all things shall be dissolved, the believer should give himself to holy living and godliness. The witnesses note the all dissolved: "seeing then that all these things shall be dissolved" — Gill notes the certain dissolution by fire; the all dissolved; the dissolution; the by fire; the certain; the melted. The expositors observe the practical use: Matthew Henry notes the apostle applies the doctrine; the practical use; the applies; the the doctrine; the the last things; the use. The witnesses press the what manner: "what manner of persons ought ye to be" — the searching question; the what manner; the the question; the ought ye; the searching; the holy. The expositors draw the lesson on the holy conversation: "in all holy conversation and godliness" — holy conduct and godliness; the holy conversation; the holy conduct; the godliness; the set apart; the to God. The witnesses observe the loose to earth: how loose should the believer sit to passing things; the loose to earth; the passing things; the sit loose; the earthly; the let go. The expositors note the spur to holiness: the doctrine is a spur to holiness; the spur to holiness; the the doctrine; the powerful; the heavenly-minded; the holy. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the seeing then that all these things shall be dissolved, the what manner of persons ought ye to be in all holy conversation and godliness, and the holy living. The verse thus records the holy living that the dissolution should produce — teaching that, since all things shall be dissolved, the believer should give himself to holy living and godliness.
12:
"Looking for and hasting unto the coming of the day of God, wherein the heavens being on fire shall be dissolved, and the elements shall melt with fervent heat?" The witnesses note the believer's looking for and hastening the day of God. Gill notes "the day of God" is the same with the day of the Lord, intending the day of Christ's second coming to judgment, and is a proof of the deity of Christ, being called "the day of God." The witnesses note Peter charging that they look for and hasten unto the coming of the day of God, in which the heavens shall be dissolved and the elements melt; the eager expectation and hastening of the day of God's coming — that Peter sets the right posture of the holy life toward the end: "looking for and hasting unto the coming of the day of God" (not dreading but eagerly expecting it, and "hasting" — earnestly desiring it, longing for it, and hastening it by prayer and holy living) — "wherein the heavens being on fire shall be dissolved, and the elements shall melt with fervent heat"; the witnesses note the title "the day of God" — for it is the day of Christ's coming, here called God's day, a testimony to Christ's deity; and the believer's stance is not the scoffer's mockery nor a fearful dread, but a glad looking-for and a longing that hastens toward the day when the heavens shall be dissolved in fire; looking for and hasting unto the coming of the day of God. The witnesses note looking for and hasting unto the coming of the day of God. The witnesses note wherein the heavens being on fire shall be dissolved, and the elements shall melt with fervent heat.

The witnesses note the believer's eager looking for and hastening the day of God — not dreading but longing for it, hastening it by prayer and holy living; "the day of God" a testimony to Christ's deity.

So the witnesses make the verse the eager expectation of the day of God. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer looks for and hastens the coming day of God, not dreading but longing for it. The witnesses note the looking for: "looking for and hasting unto the coming of the day of God" — Gill notes eagerly expecting it; the looking for; the eagerly expecting; the not dreading; the the day; the awaited. The expositors observe the hasting: "and hasting" — earnestly desiring and hastening it; the hasting; the desiring; the longing; the hastening; the by prayer. The witnesses press the the day of God: "the day of God" — the day of Christ's coming, called God's day; the the day of God; the Christ's coming; the God's day; the the deity; the Christ. The expositors draw the lesson on the deity of Christ: the title is a testimony to Christ's deity; the deity of Christ; the the title; the God's day; the Christ; the divine. The witnesses observe the heavens on fire: "wherein the heavens being on fire shall be dissolved" — the heavens dissolved in fire; the heavens on fire; the dissolved; the in fire; the the elements; the melt. The expositors note the not dread but longing: the believer's stance is not dread but glad longing; the not dread but longing; the glad; the longing; the hastens; the toward the day. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the looking for and hasting unto the coming of the day of God, the wherein the heavens being on fire shall be dissolved and the elements shall melt with fervent heat, and the eager expectation. The verse thus records the eager expectation of the day of God — teaching that the believer looks for and hastens the coming day, not dreading but longing for it.
13:
"Nevertheless we, according to his promise, look for new heavens and a new earth, wherein dwelleth righteousness." The witnesses note the believer's hope of new heavens and a new earth. Barnes notes the allusion is, beyond a doubt, to two passages in Isaiah where a promise of this kind is found — "For, behold, I create new heavens and a new earth." The witnesses note Peter declaring that, according to God's promise, we look for new heavens and a new earth in which righteousness dwells; the blessed hope beyond the fire — a new world where righteousness dwells — that Peter, having shown the present world's dissolution, lifts the believer's eye past the fire to the glorious hope beyond: "nevertheless we, according to his promise" (resting on God's own promise, given by Isaiah) "look for new heavens and a new earth, wherein dwelleth righteousness"; the witnesses note the comfort — the burning up of the old is not the end, but the way to a new and better creation; the believer looks for "new heavens and a new earth," a renewed and glorified world, and its crowning glory is that "righteousness" shall "dwell" there — a world wholly cleansed of sin, the everlasting home of holiness; so that beyond the ruin of the old world lies the believer's true and abiding hope, a new creation where righteousness is at home; new heavens and a new earth, wherein dwelleth righteousness. The witnesses note we, according to his promise, look for new heavens and a new earth. The witnesses note wherein dwelleth righteousness.

The witnesses note the hope beyond the fire — new heavens and a new earth where righteousness dwells; the burning of the old not the end but the way to a new and better creation, the home of holiness.

So the witnesses make the verse the hope of a new creation where righteousness dwells. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer looks past the fire to new heavens and a new earth, the everlasting home of righteousness. The witnesses note the according to his promise: "we, according to his promise" — Barnes notes the promise given by Isaiah; the according to his promise; the the promise; the Isaiah; the God's promise; the given. The expositors observe the look for: "look for new heavens and a new earth" — the believer looks for a new world; the look for; the new heavens; the new earth; the a new world; the awaited. The witnesses press the renewed creation: a renewed and glorified world; the renewed creation; the glorified; the a new world; the better; the new. The expositors draw the lesson on the righteousness dwells: "wherein dwelleth righteousness" — its glory is that righteousness dwells there; the righteousness dwells; the the glory; the dwells; the cleansed; the holiness. The witnesses observe the not the end: the burning of the old is not the end but the way to the new; the not the end; the the burning; the the way; the the new; the better. The expositors note the home of holiness: a world wholly cleansed, the home of holiness; the home of holiness; the wholly cleansed; the of sin; the the everlasting home; the righteousness. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the we according to his promise look for new heavens and a new earth, the wherein dwelleth righteousness, and the hope of a new creation. The verse thus records the hope of a new creation where righteousness dwells — teaching that the believer looks past the fire to new heavens and a new earth, the home of righteousness.
14:
"Wherefore, beloved, seeing that ye look for such things, be diligent that ye may be found of him in peace, without spot, and blameless." The witnesses note the call to diligence and purity in view of the coming things. Barnes notes the effect of such hopes and prospects should be to lead us to be diligent in securing our salvation, to be found of him in peace. The witnesses note Peter charging that, since they look for such things, they be diligent to be found of him in peace, without spot and blameless; the diligent pursuit of peace and purity in expectation of the day — that Peter draws the practical conclusion from the hope of the new creation: "wherefore, beloved, seeing that ye look for such things" (since you expect the dissolution of the old and the coming of the new) "be diligent" (give earnest, all-out diligence) "that ye may be found of him in peace, without spot, and blameless"; the witnesses note the threefold aim — to be found, when Christ comes, "in peace" (reconciled to God and at rest with him), "without spot" (pure, unstained by sin), and "blameless" (against whom no charge can stand); so that the believer's looking for the day is to make him earnest now to be ready for it, pure and at peace, so that the great day may find him prepared; be diligent that ye may be found of him in peace, without spot, and blameless. The witnesses note seeing that ye look for such things, be diligent. The witnesses note that ye may be found of him in peace, without spot, and blameless.

The witnesses note the call to diligence and purity — that the believer be found of Christ in peace, without spot, and blameless; the looking for the day making him earnest to be ready for it.

So the witnesses make the verse the diligent pursuit of peace and purity. The lesson the expositors draw is that the hope of the day should make the believer diligent to be found in peace, pure and blameless. The witnesses note the look for such things: "seeing that ye look for such things" — Barnes notes the hope should lead to diligence; the look for such things; the the hope; the expect; the the new; the diligence. The expositors observe the be diligent: "be diligent" — give earnest, all-out diligence; the be diligent; the earnest; the all-out; the securing; the salvation. The witnesses press the found in peace: "that ye may be found of him in peace" — reconciled and at rest with God; the found in peace; the reconciled; the at rest; the with God; the peace. The expositors draw the lesson on the without spot: "without spot" — pure, unstained by sin; the without spot; the pure; the unstained; the by sin; the clean. The witnesses observe the blameless: "and blameless" — against whom no charge can stand; the blameless; the no charge; the can stand; the unblamable; the clean. The expositors note the ready for the day: the believer is to be ready and prepared for the day; the ready for the day; the prepared; the earnest; the the day; the found. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the seeing that ye look for such things be diligent, the that ye may be found of him in peace without spot and blameless, and the diligent purity. The verse thus records the diligent pursuit of peace and purity — teaching that the hope of the day should make the believer diligent to be found in peace, pure and blameless.
15:
"And account that the longsuffering of our Lord is salvation; even as our beloved brother Paul also according to the wisdom given unto him hath written unto you;" The witnesses note that the Lord's longsuffering is to be counted salvation, as Paul also wrote. Gill notes this is not the longsuffering toward the wicked, which issues in their greater damnation, but toward the elect, whom the Lord bears with long before conversion; this longsuffering is salvation. The witnesses note Peter charging that they account the longsuffering of the Lord to be salvation, as Paul also wrote according to the wisdom given him; the Lord's patience reckoned as the very means of salvation, confirmed by Paul — that Peter, returning to God's longsuffering (v.9), bids them rightly interpret it: "account that the longsuffering of our Lord is salvation" (reckon the Lord's patient delay not as slackness but as the very space and means of salvation, the time in which his people are brought to repentance) — and he confirms this by the witness of a fellow-apostle: "even as our beloved brother Paul also according to the wisdom given unto him hath written unto you"; the witnesses note the warmth and the weight — Peter calls Paul "our beloved brother," owning him as a fellow-labourer who wrote "according to the wisdom given unto him" (by divine inspiration), and whose epistles taught the same truths; so that the Lord's longsuffering is the season of salvation, as both Peter and Paul agree; the longsuffering of our Lord is salvation. The witnesses note account that the longsuffering of our Lord is salvation. The witnesses note even as our beloved brother Paul also according to the wisdom given unto him hath written unto you.

The witnesses note the Lord's longsuffering reckoned as salvation — the space in which his people are brought to repentance — confirmed by "our beloved brother Paul," who wrote by the wisdom given him.

So the witnesses make the verse the Lord's patience as the means of salvation. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Lord's longsuffering is the space of salvation, as both Peter and Paul taught. The witnesses note the longsuffering is salvation: "account that the longsuffering of our Lord is salvation" — Gill notes the patient delay is the space of salvation; the longsuffering is salvation; the patient delay; the the space; the not slackness; the repentance. The expositors observe the the elect: toward the elect, brought to repentance; the the elect; the brought; the to repentance; the his people; the borne. The witnesses press the as Paul wrote: "even as our beloved brother Paul also... hath written" — confirmed by Paul; the as Paul wrote; the confirmed; the Paul; the the apostle; the agrees. The expositors draw the lesson on the beloved brother: "our beloved brother Paul" — Peter owns Paul as a fellow-labourer; the beloved brother; the fellow-labourer; the owns; the Paul; the warm. The witnesses observe the wisdom given: "according to the wisdom given unto him" — by divine inspiration; the wisdom given; the divine inspiration; the given him; the the wisdom; the inspired. The expositors note the same truths: Paul's epistles taught the same truths; the same truths; the Paul's epistles; the taught; the agree; the the same. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the account that the longsuffering of our Lord is salvation, the even as our beloved brother Paul also according to the wisdom given unto him hath written unto you, and the Lord's patience as salvation. The verse thus records the Lord's patience as the means of salvation — teaching that the Lord's longsuffering is the space of salvation, as both Peter and Paul taught.
16:
"As also in all his epistles, speaking in them of these things; in which are some things hard to be understood, which they that are unlearned and unstable wrest, as they do also the other scriptures, unto their own destruction." The witnesses note Paul's epistles ranked with Scripture, and the danger of wresting them. Gill notes from this it appears that Paul had by this time written several of his epistles, if not all, written according to the same wisdom and under the influence of the same Spirit as his epistle to the Hebrews. The witnesses note Peter observing that in all Paul's epistles, speaking of these things, are some things hard to be understood, which the unlearned and unstable wrest, as they do the other Scriptures, to their own destruction; Paul's epistles owned as Scripture, and the peril of twisting the hard parts — that Peter sets Paul's letters alongside the Old Testament as sacred Scripture, and warns of their abuse: "as also in all his epistles, speaking in them of these things" (Paul wrote of these same truths throughout his letters) "in which are some things hard to be understood" (certain deep and difficult passages) "which they that are unlearned and unstable wrest, as they do also the other scriptures, unto their own destruction"; the witnesses note two weighty things — first, Peter ranks "all his epistles" with "the other scriptures," a clear witness that Paul's writings are inspired Scripture; and second, the warning that the untaught and unsettled twist and distort the hard passages of Scripture (Paul's and others') to their own ruin; so that the difficulty lies not in the word but in the corrupt handling of it by the unstable; which they that are unlearned and unstable wrest... unto their own destruction. The witnesses note in which are some things hard to be understood. The witnesses note which they that are unlearned and unstable wrest, as they do also the other scriptures, unto their own destruction.

The witnesses note Paul's epistles ranked with Scripture, and the peril of wresting the hard parts — the unlearned and unstable twisting them, as they do the other Scriptures, to their own ruin.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's epistles as Scripture and the danger of wresting them. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul's letters are inspired Scripture, and the unstable twist their hard parts, as they do all Scripture, to their own destruction. The witnesses note the all his epistles: "as also in all his epistles" — Gill notes Paul wrote of these things throughout; the all his epistles; the throughout; the wrote; the the letters. The expositors observe the hard to understand: "some things hard to be understood" — certain deep, difficult passages; the hard to understand; the deep; the difficult; the certain. The witnesses press the wrest: "which they that are unlearned and unstable wrest" — the untaught twist them; the wrest; the twist; the the unstable; the distort. The expositors draw the lesson on the other scriptures: "as they do also the other scriptures" — ranking Paul's letters with the Scriptures; the the other scriptures; the ranking; the Paul's letters; the with Scripture; the inspired. The witnesses observe the own destruction: "unto their own destruction" — to their own ruin; the own destruction; the ruin; the lost. The expositors note the not the word: the fault lies not in the word but in the handling; the not the word; the corrupt; the the fault. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the which they that are unlearned and unstable wrest as they do also the other scriptures unto their own destruction, and Paul's epistles as Scripture. The verse thus records Paul's epistles as Scripture and the danger of wresting them — teaching that Paul's letters are inspired Scripture, and the unstable twist their hard parts to their own destruction.
17:
"Ye therefore, beloved, seeing ye know these things before, beware lest ye also, being led away with the error of the wicked, fall from your own stedfastness." The witnesses note the warning to beware of being led away by error. Barnes notes, being aware of this danger and knowing that such results would follow, beware; Gill notes they knew beforehand there would be mockers and unlearned men who would wrest the Scriptures. The witnesses note Peter warning the beloved, since they know these things beforehand, to beware lest, led away by the error of the wicked, they fall from their steadfastness; the warning to the forewarned not to be carried away from steadfastness by error — that Peter applies the whole to his readers' watchfulness: "ye therefore, beloved, seeing ye know these things before" (being forewarned of the scoffers and the wresting of Scripture) "beware" (be on your guard) "lest ye also, being led away with the error of the wicked, fall from your own stedfastness"; the witnesses note the peril and the safeguard — the danger is real, that even the forewarned may be "led away" by the plausible "error of the wicked" and so "fall from" their "own stedfastness" (their firm standing in the faith); so that knowledge of the danger is given precisely that they may watch and not be swept from their settled footing in the truth; beware lest ye also, being led away with the error of the wicked, fall from your own stedfastness. The witnesses note seeing ye know these things before, beware. The witnesses note lest ye also, being led away with the error of the wicked, fall from your own stedfastness.

The witnesses note the warning to the forewarned — to beware lest, led away by the error of the wicked, they fall from their steadfastness; knowledge of the danger given that they may watch.

So the witnesses make the verse the warning not to be led away from steadfastness. The lesson the expositors draw is that the forewarned must beware, lest the error of the wicked lead them from their firm standing in the faith. The witnesses note the know before: "seeing ye know these things before" — Gill notes forewarned of the scoffers and wresting; the know before; the forewarned; the the scoffers; the the wresting; the warned. The expositors observe the beware: "beware" — be on your guard; the beware; the on guard; the watch; the careful; the warned. The witnesses press the led away: "lest ye also, being led away" — even the forewarned may be carried away; the led away; the carried away; the the forewarned; the swept; the seduced. The expositors draw the lesson on the error of the wicked: "with the error of the wicked" — the plausible error of the wicked; the error of the wicked; the plausible; the the error; the the wicked; the false. The witnesses observe the fall from steadfastness: "fall from your own stedfastness" — fall from their firm standing; the fall from steadfastness; the firm standing; the in the faith; the settled; the fall. The expositors note the watch: knowledge is given that they may watch and not fall; the watch; the the danger; the not fall; the the safeguard; the steadfast. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the seeing ye know these things before beware, the lest ye also being led away with the error of the wicked fall from your own stedfastness, and the warning to watch. The verse thus records the warning not to be led away from steadfastness — teaching that the forewarned must beware, lest the error of the wicked lead them from their firm standing.
18:
"But grow in grace, and in the knowledge of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ. To him be glory both now and for ever. Amen." The witnesses note the closing charge to grow in grace and the doxology to Christ. Gill notes "grow in grace," in the gifts of grace, which under a divine blessing may be increased by using them; gifts neglected decrease, but stirred up and used are improved. The witnesses note Peter charging that they grow in grace and in the knowledge of Christ, and closing with the doxology, "To him be glory both now and for ever. Amen"; the positive safeguard of growth in grace, sealed with glory to Christ — that Peter ends the epistle with the great preservative against falling (v.17): not standing still, but going forward — "but grow in grace, and in the knowledge of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ"; the witnesses note the twofold growth — in "grace" (in faith, love, holiness, and all the graces of the Spirit) and in "the knowledge of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ" (an ever-deepening acquaintance with Christ himself), the surest guard against being led away by error; and he crowns the whole letter with a doxology to Christ: "to him be glory both now and for ever. Amen" — ascribing to the Lord and Saviour eternal glory, a fitting witness to his deity; to him be glory both now and for ever. Amen. The witnesses note but grow in grace, and in the knowledge of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ. The witnesses note to him be glory both now and for ever. Amen.

The witnesses note the closing charge to grow in grace and in the knowledge of Christ — the surest guard against error — sealed with a doxology to Christ, a fitting witness to his deity.

So the witnesses make the verse the growth in grace and the doxology to Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer's safeguard is growth in grace and the knowledge of Christ, the letter closing with glory to him. The witnesses note the grow in grace: "but grow in grace" — Gill notes growth in the graces, by using them; the grow in grace; the the graces; the increased; the by using; the growth. The expositors observe the not standing still: not standing still but going forward; the not standing still; the going forward; the growth; the advance; the the safeguard. The witnesses press the knowledge of Christ: "and in the knowledge of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ" — an ever-deepening knowledge of Christ; the knowledge of Christ; the deepening; the acquaintance; the Christ; the himself. The expositors draw the lesson on the guard against error: this is the surest guard against being led away; the guard against error; the the surest; the led away; the the safeguard; the growth. The witnesses observe the doxology: "to him be glory both now and for ever" — glory ascribed to Christ; the doxology; the glory; the to Christ; the now and for ever; the praise. The expositors note the his deity: the doxology to Christ is a witness to his deity; the his deity; the the doxology; the to Christ; the divine; the witness. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the but grow in grace and in the knowledge of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ, the to him be glory both now and for ever Amen, and the growth and doxology. The verse thus records the growth in grace and the doxology to Christ — teaching that the believer's safeguard is growth in grace and the knowledge of Christ, the letter closing with glory to him.
